1.pericope_label: Praise the Lord, for He Is Good
1.synthesis: Psalm 135 opens with a threefold command to praise that sets up the whole psalm’s movement from exhortation to reason to illustration: “Praise the Lord! Praise the name of the Lord, give praise, O servants of the Lord, who stand in the house of the Lord, in the courts of the house of our God! Praise the Lord, for the Lord is good; sing to his name, for it is pleasant!” (Ps 135:1–3). The opening section provides the foundational reason for praise: “For the Lord has chosen Jacob for himself, Israel as his own possession” (135:4). The goodness and pleasantness of YHWH’s name is not an abstract aesthetic claim but a relational one: it is grounded in the specific act of election by which YHWH chose Israel to be his particular treasure. The servants standing in the house of the Lord echo the nighttime servants of Psalm 134, and Psalm 135 may function as a liturgical expansion of the themes compressed in that closing Ascent psalm.
1.historical_context: The command to praise YHWH’s name (135:1) draws on the theological significance of the divine name as the accessible form of YHWH’s personal identity in worship. The courts of the house of the Lord (135:2) locate this praise in the temple precincts where both priests and laity could gather, suggesting a worship context that included the whole assembly. The language of election in verse 4—Israel as YHWH’s “own possession” (segullah)—is drawn from Exodus 19:5, where YHWH at Sinai declared Israel his special treasure among all peoples. The psalm is rich in textual echoes: it borrows from Psalm 115, 136, and Deuteronomic traditions, suggesting it is a deliberately composed compilation that draws Israel’s liturgical heritage into a single act of praise.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 135 is organized into three sections that correspond to three grounds for praise: YHWH’s goodness and election (vv. 1–4), YHWH’s greatness in creation and history (vv. 5–12), and the contrast between YHWH and the worthless idols (vv. 13–21). The first section establishes the relational context (servants in the house, chosen people) before moving to the cosmic demonstrations of YHWH’s greatness. The structure follows a consistent psalmic pattern: invitation to praise followed by reasons, with each reason expanding the scope of YHWH’s activity from the intimate (election) to the cosmic (creation and history) to the polemical (idols). The opening section’s brevity—four verses before the psalm expands into its demonstrations—suggests that election is the most compact and foundational reason from which everything else follows.
1.theological_application: The connection between YHWH’s goodness and his election of Israel (135:4) teaches something about the basis of praise: praise is not a response to YHWH’s general goodness in the abstract but to the specific goodness of having been chosen. Communities shaped by this opening section learn to praise from within the particular story of their election—not celebrating YHWH as a universal principle but thanking the God who chose them in Christ before the foundation of the world. The servants who stand in YHWH’s house (135:2) are not worshipping a God of their own making but one who has placed them where they are; the praise flows from the recognition that their standing in his house is itself a gift of his election rather than their achievement.
1.christology: The election of Israel as YHWH’s own possession (135:4) is the type of the election of the church in Christ: “he chose us in him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and blameless before him. In love he predestined us for adoption” (Eph 1:4–5). The name of YHWH that is praised (135:1) is the name above every name given to Christ: “so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow” (Phil 2:10). Psalm 135’s praise for the goodness of YHWH’s name is therefore praise for the name in which alone salvation is found (Acts 4:12)—the chosen Israel’s praise becoming the chorus of every tribe and tongue and nation who are gathered in Christ as YHWH’s new possession.

5.synthesis: The second section of Psalm 135 expands from YHWH’s election of Israel to his greatness over all creation and history: “For I know that the Lord is great, and that our Lord is above all gods” (Ps 135:5). The evidence unfolds in two registers. First, creation: “Whatever the Lord pleases, he does, in heaven and on earth, in the seas and all deeps. He it is who makes the clouds rise at the end of the earth, who makes lightnings for the rain and brings forth the wind from his storehouses” (135:6–7). Second, history: “He it is who struck down the firstborn of Egypt, both of humans and of animals; who in your midst, O Egypt, sent signs and wonders against Pharaoh and all his servants; who struck down many nations and killed mighty kings” (135:8–10). The section closes with the gift of the land: “and gave their land as a heritage, a heritage to Israel his people” (135:12).
5.historical_context: The second section of Psalm 135 is a rapid survey of YHWH’s acts in creation and Exodus-conquest history, drawing on stock passages and formulaic language familiar from Deuteronomy, the Psalms, and the prophets. The “storehouses” of wind (135:7) appear in Jeremiah 10:13 and 51:16, suggesting this language was part of the liturgical and prophetic vocabulary of YHWH’s cosmic mastery. The Exodus plagues against Egypt (vv. 8–9) and the defeat of Sihon and Og (v. 11) are the standard summary of YHWH’s historical acts on Israel’s behalf, appearing also in Psalm 136 and Deuteronomy 2–3. The purpose of the survey is not mere historical recitation but praise: each act is evidence that YHWH is “above all gods” (v. 5)—the gods of Egypt could not prevent the plagues, and the gods of Canaan could not save their kings.
5.literary_structure: The second section of Psalm 135 moves from the general (“the Lord is great,” v. 5) to the specific (creation phenomena, Exodus events, conquest of kings) in a demonstration that fills out the initial claim with concrete evidence. The pattern of “he it is who” (vv. 7, 8, 10) creates a rhythmic attribution of each act to YHWH’s direct agency, preventing the historicizing move that treats these events as merely natural or political. The section ends with the land as heritage (v. 12), which connects the historical demonstration back to the election of verse 4: YHWH who chose Israel for himself worked through creation and history to give his chosen people their inheritance.
5.theological_application: The survey of YHWH’s acts in creation and history teaches a practice of theological attention: to look at the natural world (clouds, lightning, wind) and the course of history (Exodus, conquest) and see in them the direct agency of YHWH rather than impersonal processes or human achievement. Communities shaped by this section cultivate the habit of reading the world as YHWH’s theater of action, in which every storm and every political development is an opportunity to recognize the one who does whatever he pleases in heaven and earth and sea. The declaration “our Lord is above all gods” (135:5) is not a concession that other gods exist but a declaration that anything claiming divine authority is answerable to YHWH’s sovereign pleasure.
5.christology: YHWH who does whatever he pleases in heaven and on earth (135:6) is the sovereign will that the New Testament identifies with Christ: “All things were created through him and for him. And he is before all things, and in him all things hold together” (Col 1:16–17). The Exodus acts—striking the firstborn, sending signs and wonders—are the acts of the God who in Christ performs the greater exodus: “he has delivered us from the domain of darkness and transferred us to the kingdom of his beloved Son, in whom we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins” (Col 1:13–14). The heritage given to Israel (135:12) is the type of the inheritance Christ secures for his people: “an inheritance that is imperishable, undefiled, and unfading, kept in heaven for you” (1 Pet 1:4).

13.synthesis: The third section of Psalm 135 shifts from YHWH’s greatness to the zero-value of the nations’ idols: “The idols of the nations are silver and gold, the work of human hands. They have mouths, but do not speak; they have eyes, but do not see; they have ears, but do not hear, nor is there any breath in their mouths. Those who make them become like them, so do all who trust in them!” (Ps 135:15–18). The section opens with YHWH’s perpetual name and reputation: “Your name, O Lord, endures forever, your renown, O Lord, throughout all ages” (135:13). Between the eternal name and the dumb idols stands the pastoral declaration of verse 14: “For the Lord will vindicate his people and have compassion on his servants.” The section closes with the communal summons: “Bless the Lord, O house of Israel! Bless the Lord, O house of Aaron! Bless the Lord, O house of Levi!… Praise the Lord!” (135:19–21).
13.historical_context: The idol polemic of Psalm 135:15–18 is closely paralleled in Psalm 115:4–8 and draws on a sustained biblical tradition of satirizing idol manufacture that culminates in Isaiah’s extended parody in Isaiah 44:9–20. The specific list—mouths that do not speak, eyes that do not see, ears that do not hear, no breath in the mouth—is an anatomy of spiritual incapacity: the idol has all the sensory equipment of the living God but none of the function. The most devastating line is the conclusion: those who make them and trust them become like them, spiritually inert, unable to truly hear or see. The closing summons to the house of Israel, Aaron, Levi, and those who fear YHWH (135:19–20) is deliberately inclusive—gathering every category of worshipper (tribal, priestly, Levitical, and proselyte) into the act of communal blessing.
13.literary_structure: The third section of Psalm 135 is organized around a contrast between YHWH’s enduring name and reputation (vv. 13–14) and the temporal worthlessness of the nations’ idols (vv. 15–18), followed by the communal summons to bless YHWH (vv. 19–21). The placement of the vindication declaration (v. 14) before the idol polemic is significant: YHWH’s compassion for his people is the context in which the idol polemic makes its pastoral point—those who trust in idols receive the incapacity of their idols, while those who trust in YHWH receive his vindication and compassion. The closing summons repeats the structure of verse 2 (servants of the Lord standing in his house) but expands it into explicit categories, so that the psalm’s ending is both a call to praise and a roll-call of the whole worshipping community.
13.theological_application: The idol polemic’s most theologically pointed line—“those who make them become like them” (135:18)—expresses a principle of moral and spiritual formation: we become what we worship. The idol of silver and gold has no breath, no voice, no perception; those who entrust themselves to it gradually take on its incapacities—spiritually mute, spiritually blind, spiritually deaf. The inverse is also true, though the psalm leaves it implicit: those who worship YHWH—who sees everything, hears every prayer, and speaks with power—are formed in the image of a God who is truly alive. Communities shaped by this section develop discernment about what functions as an idol in their common life: any object of ultimate trust that cannot speak, see, or deliver is in the category the psalmist names as making its worshippers like itself.
13.christology: The contrast between YHWH’s enduring name and the mute idols (135:13–18) is concentrated in Christ as the Word who speaks: “In the beginning was the Word” (John 1:1)—where idols have mouths that do not speak, Christ is the very speech of God made flesh. The formation principle of verse 18 (“those who make them become like them”) is reversed in Christ: those who are conformed to the image of the Son become like the God who sees, hears, and acts: “And we all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another” (2 Cor 3:18). The vindication YHWH promises his servants (135:14) is accomplished in the resurrection, where the servant who was executed is vindicated by YHWH’s own act of raising him from the dead.
